Consider $\lambda^{1 / 2} \leq 0.4$, where $\operatorname{erf}\left(\lambda^{1 / 2}\right) / \lambda^{1 / 2}$ approaches the limiting value of $2 / \pi^{1 / 2}$ (within ca. $5 \%$ ) and (12.3.16) yields $\left(i \tau^{1 / 2} / i \tau_{\mathrm{d}}^{1 / 2}\right) \approx K /(1+K)$, which is the diffusion-controlled response corrected by calculat-

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-493.jpg?height=478&width=934&top_left_y=1748&top_left_x=144)
Figure 12.3.7 Variation of $i \tau^{1 / 2} / i \tau_{d}^{1 / 2}$ with $\lambda$ for various values of $K$ (indicated on curve) for chronopotentiometric study of the $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}}$ reaction scheme.

ing $C_{\mathrm{O}}(x, 0)$ from $C^{*}$. Thus this condition, or $\log \lambda<-0.8$, defines the left boundary (line 1). For large $\lambda$ (e.g., $\lambda^{1 / 2} \geq 1.4$ ), $\operatorname{erf}\left(\lambda^{1 / 2}\right) \approx 1$ and (12.3.16) yields

$$
\frac{i \tau^{1 / 2}}{i \tau_{\mathrm{d}}^{1 / 2}}=\left(1+\frac{0.886}{K \lambda^{1 / 2}}\right)^{-1}
$$

This condition gives diffusion-controlled behavior when $0.886 / K \lambda^{1 / 2} \leq 0.05$, or log $K=1.25-(1 / 2) \log \lambda$; this represents the right boundary (line 2 ). The pure kinetic region is also defined by large $\lambda$ values, this time as $K \rightarrow 0$. One can set the boundary by using $\lambda^{1 / 2} \geq 1.4$ (line 3 ) and the condition that the second term on the right predominates in (12.3.17). Thus $0.886 / K \lambda^{1 / 2} \geq 10$ or $\log K=-1 / 2 \log \lambda-1.05$ (line 4). Note that the exact locations of these boundaries depend on the levels of approximations used. Moreover, in this pure kinetic region, (12.3.14) becomes

$$
i \tau^{1 / 2}=i \tau_{\mathrm{d}}^{1 / 2}-\frac{i \pi^{1 / 2}}{2 K\left(k_{\mathrm{f}}+k_{\mathrm{b}}\right)^{1 / 2}}
$$

so that a plot of $i \tau^{1 / 2} \nu s . i$ in this region is a straight line of slope $-\pi^{1 / 2} / 2 K\left(k_{\mathrm{f}}+k_{\mathrm{b}}\right)^{1 / 2}$. This behavior is evident in the plots shown in Figure 12.3.8.

For simple reversal chronopotentiometry, the ratio of reversal transition time $\tau_{2}$ to the forward time $\tau_{1}$ is $1 / 3$, just as in the diffusion-controlled case, independent of the rate constants. However, for cyclic chronopotentiometry the transition times for the third ( $\tau_{3}$ ) and subsequent reversals differ from those of the diffusion-controlled case (31).

### 12.3.2 Preceding Reaction- $\mathbf{C}_{\mathbf{r}} \mathbf{E}_{\mathbf{i}}$

This scheme is the same as that in Section 12.3.1, except that the electron-transfer reaction (12.3.2) is totally irreversible and is governed by the charge-transfer parameters $\alpha$ and $k^{0}$. The limiting current behavior in chronoamperometric and polarographic methods will not be perturbed by irreversibility in the electron-transfer reaction; since the potential is stepped to a value sufficiently beyond the equilibrium value that reaction (12.3.2) proceeds rapidly. Thus the results will be the same as in Section 12.3.1(b). This case illustrates an important advantage of chronoamperometric methods: that the potential can be chosen to eliminate complexities in the analysis of the behavior caused by the heterogeneous electron-transfer

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-494.jpg?height=664&width=758&top_left_y=1639&top_left_x=199)
Figure 12.3.8 Variation of $i \tau^{1 / 2}$ with $i$, for various values of ( $k_{\mathrm{f}}+k_{\mathrm{b}}$ ) (in s ${ }^{-1}$ ). Calculated for $K=0.1, C^{*}=0.11 \mathrm{~m} M$, and $D=10^{-5} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$. [Reprinted with permission from P. Delahay and T. Berzins, J. Am. Chem. Soc., 75, 2486 (1953). Copyright 1953, American Chemical Society.]

step. On the other hand, once the rate constants of the homogeneous reactions have been deduced, potential steps to less extreme potentials can provide information about $\alpha$ and $k^{0}$. This requires solution of the more complex problem where the boundary condition for $C_{\mathrm{O}}(0$, $t$ ) is governed by the heterogeneous reaction rate. This problem will not be examined here.

The $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{i}}$ case has been treated for linear sweep voltammetry (25). Because of the irreversibility of the electron transfer, no anodic current is observed on the reverse scan and cyclic voltammetric behavior need not be considered. Typical $i-E$ curves are shown in Figure 12.3.9. The limiting behavior again depends on the magnitude of the kinetic parameter $K \lambda_{\mathrm{i}}^{1 / 2}$, where $\lambda_{\mathrm{i}}$ is the $\lambda$ factor of Table 12.3.1, with $n$ set to $\alpha n=\alpha$ for a one-electron " E " step:

$$
\lambda_{\mathrm{i}}=\frac{k_{\mathrm{f}}+k_{\mathrm{b}}}{v}\left(\frac{R T}{\alpha F}\right)
$$

When $\lambda_{\mathrm{i}}$ is small, the behavior is the same as that of the unperturbed irreversible one-step, one-electron reaction, as described in Section 6.3, except that the concentration of O is given by $C^{*}[K /(1+K)]$. This represents the limiting behavior at high scan rates. For large $\lambda_{\mathrm{i}}$ and large values of $K \lambda_{\mathrm{i}}^{1 / 2}$, the preceding reaction can be considered to be essentially at equilibrium at all times, and again the $i-E$ behavior becomes that of the unperturbed irreversible case in Section 6.3, with the wave shifted (from the position it would have had without the preceding reaction) in a negative direction by an amount $(R T / \alpha F) \ln [K /(1+ K)]$. For small values of $K \lambda_{\mathrm{i}}^{1 / 2}$ (but with large $\lambda_{\mathrm{i}}$ ), the behavior depends on $k^{0}$, as well as $K$ and $\lambda_{\mathrm{i}}$, and the $i-E$ curve no longer shows a peak, but instead has an S-shape with a current plateau. This is the pure kinetic region where the limiting current becomes independent of $v$, as in the case of the $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}}$ scheme. Under these conditions the current is given by (25)

$$
i=\frac{F A C^{*} D^{1 / 2} K\left(k_{\mathrm{f}}+k_{\mathrm{b}}\right)^{1 / 2}}{1+\left[\frac{\pi D^{1 / 2} \alpha f v(K+1)}{k^{0}\left(k_{\mathrm{f}}+k_{\mathrm{b}}\right)^{1 / 2}}\right] \exp \left[\alpha f\left(E-E^{0^{\prime}}\right)\right]}
$$

Nicholson and Shain (25) suggest that for all ranges of $K \lambda_{\mathrm{i}}^{1 / 2}$ the kinetic parameters can be obtained by fitting the kinetic peak (or plateau) current for the $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{i}}$ case, $i_{\mathrm{k}}$, to that for the diffusion-controlled peak current for an irreversible charge transfer, $i_{\mathrm{d}}$ (equation 6.3.12), by the empirical equation

$$
\frac{i_{\mathrm{k}}}{i_{\mathrm{d}}}=\frac{1}{1.02+0.531 / K \sqrt{\lambda_{i}}}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-495.jpg?height=583&width=500&top_left_y=1724&top_left_x=140)
Figure 12.3.9 Curves of current [plotted as $\pi^{1 / 2} \chi(b t)$, where $\chi(b t)$ is defined as in (6.3.6)] vs. potential at different values of $\left(K \lambda_{\mathrm{i}}^{1 / 2}\right)^{-1}$ (shown on curves). The potential scale is $\alpha\left(E-E^{0^{\prime}}\right)+(R T / F) \ln \left[(\pi D b)^{1 / 2} / k^{0}\right)-(R T / F) \ln [K /(1+K)] . b=\alpha F v / R T ; \lambda_{\mathrm{i}}=\left(k_{\mathrm{f}}+k_{\mathrm{b}}\right) / \mathrm{b}$. [Reprinted with permission from R. S. Nicholson and I. Shain, Anal. Chem., 36, 706 (1964). Copyright 1964, American Chemical Society.]

[compare to (12.3.9)]. For the more general $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{q}}$ case, the best approach is via digital simulation for different values of the parameters.

For chronopotentiometry, the behavior of $\tau$ is again like the $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}}$ case, since the wave will shift to sufficiently negative values to maintain the electron-transfer reaction rate at the value required by the applied constant current, and the treatment in Section 12.3.1 applies.

### 12.3.3 Following Reaction- $\mathbf{E}_{\mathbf{r}} \mathbf{C}_{\mathbf{i}}$

This case for the chronopotentiometric method was treated in Section 12.2.2, and the zones for pure diffusion behavior (DP) and pure kinetic behavior (KP) were derived in terms of the dimensionless kinetic parameter $\lambda$ (Table 12.3.1): DP, $\lambda<0.1 ; \mathrm{KP}, \lambda>5$ (Figure 12.2.1). These zones generally apply with the other techniques as well.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-496.jpg?height=1231&width=1192&top_left_y=843&top_left_x=190)
Figure 12.3.10 Cyclic voltammograms for $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}$ case at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C} . \mathrm{A}+e \rightleftarrows \mathrm{~B} ; \mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{C} .(a-d)$ System where $E_{\mathrm{A} / \mathrm{B}}^{0}=0 \mathrm{~V}, C_{\mathrm{A}}^{*}=1 \mathrm{~m} M, C_{\mathrm{B}}^{*}=0, A=1 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}, D_{\mathrm{A}}=D_{\mathrm{B}}=10^{-5} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$, and $k_{\mathrm{f}}=10 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ at scan rates $v$ of $(a) 10,(b) 1,(c) 0.1$, and $(d) 0.01 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{s}$. Current in amperes; potential in mV . Note that the vertical scale changes from panel to panel. (e) Normalized current for several values of $\lambda=k R T / n F v$. [Part (e) reprinted with permission from R. S. Nicholson and I. Shain, Anal. Chem., 36, 706 (1964). Copyright 1964, American Chemical Society.]

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-497.jpg?height=422&width=565&top_left_y=217&top_left_x=144)
Figure 12.3.11 Variation of peak potential as a function of $\lambda$ for the $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}$ case. [Reprinted with permission from R. S. Nicholson and I. Shain, Anal. Chem., 36, 706 (1964). Copyright 1964, American Chemical Society.]

## (a) Linear Sweep and Cyclic Voltammetric Methods

Typical curves for this case, both as they would appear in an experimental trial and in normalized form, are given in Figure 12.3.10. At small values of $\lambda$, essentially reversible behavior is found. For large values of $\lambda$ (in the KP region), no current is observed on scan reversal and the shape of the curve is similar to that of a totally irreversible charge transfer, (6.3.6). In this region the current function changes only slightly with scan rate (i.e., $i_{\mathrm{p}} / v^{1 / 2}$ increases by about $5 \%$ for $\lambda$ changing from 1 to 10 ). The peak, which is generally positive of the reversible $E_{\mathrm{p}}$ value because of the following reaction, shifts in a negative direction (toward the reversible curve) with increasing $v$ (Figure 12.3.11).

In the KP region, $E_{\mathrm{p}}$ is given by

$$
E_{\mathrm{p}}=E_{1 / 2}-\frac{R T}{n F} 0.780+\frac{R T}{2 n F} \ln \lambda
$$

so that the wave shifts toward negative potentials by about $30 / n \mathrm{mV}$ (at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) for a tenfold increase in $v$. In the intermediate region of $\lambda(\mathrm{KO})$, that is, $5>\lambda>0.1$, information can be obtained from the ratio of anodic and cathodic peak currents $i_{\mathrm{pa}} / i_{\mathrm{pc}}$; these are determined as described in Section 6.5.1. Nicholson and Shain (25) plotted the ratio $i_{\mathrm{pa}} / i_{\mathrm{pc}}$ as a function of $k \tau$, where $\tau$ is the time between $E_{1 / 2}$ and the switching potential $E_{\lambda}$. By fitting the observed values to a working curve (Figure 12.3.12), a value of $k_{\mathrm{f}}$ can be estimated (assuming $E_{1 / 2}$ can be determined by experiments at sufficiently high scan rates). Note, however, that reversal data yield kinetic information only over a small range of $\lambda$. It is sometimes useful to extend the scan to more extreme potentials to see if the products of the following reaction are electroactive. For example, Figure 12.3.13 shows the cyclic voltammograms for the same reaction and conditions as in Figure 12.3.10b, but with the scan extended to more positive potentials to show waves for the oxidation of species C and, on the second reversal, the reduction of its oxidation product, D .

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-497.jpg?height=445&width=628&top_left_y=1861&top_left_x=137)
Figure 12.3.12 Ratio of anodic to cathodic peak current as a function of $k_{\mathrm{f}} \tau$, where $\tau$ is the time between $E_{1 / 2}$ and the switching potential $E_{\lambda}$. [Reprinted with permission from R. S. Nicholson and I. Shain, Anal. Chem., 36, 706 (1964). Copyright 1964, American Chemical Society.]

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-498.jpg?height=565&width=779&top_left_y=236&top_left_x=176)
Figure 12.3.13 Cyclic voltammograms for the $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}$ case. $\mathrm{A}+e \rightleftarrows \mathrm{~B} ; \mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}$ (as in Figure $12.3 .10 b, v=1 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{s}$ ), with the scan extended to show the waves for the couple $\mathrm{D}+e=\mathrm{C}$, $E_{\mathrm{D} / \mathrm{C}}^{0}=0.5 \mathrm{~V}$.

## (b) Chronoamperometric Methods

Since the forward reaction for a potential step to the limiting current region is unperturbed by the irreversible following reaction, no kinetic information can be obtained from the polarographic diffusion current or the limiting chronoamperometric $i-t$ curve. Some kinetic information is contained in the rising portion of the $i-E$ wave and the shift of $E_{1 / 2}$ with $t_{\text {max }}$. Since this behavior is similar to that found in linear potential sweep methods, these results will not be described separately. The reaction rate constant $k$ can be obtained by reversal techniques (see Section 5.7) (32, 33). A convenient approach is the potential step method, where at $t=0$ the potential is stepped to a potential where $C_{\mathrm{O}}(x=0)=0$, and at $t=\tau$ it is stepped to a potential where $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{R}}(x=0)=0$. The equation for the ratio of $i_{\mathrm{a}}$ (measured at time $t_{\mathrm{r}}$ ) to $i_{\mathrm{c}}$ (measured at time $t_{\mathrm{f}}=t_{\mathrm{r}}-\tau$ ) (see Figure 5.7.3) is

$$
-\frac{i_{\mathrm{a}}}{i_{\mathrm{c}}}=\phi\left[k \tau,\left(t_{\mathrm{r}}-\tau\right) / \tau\right]-\left[\frac{\left(t_{\mathrm{r}}-\tau\right) / \tau}{1+\left(t_{\mathrm{r}}-\tau\right) / \tau}\right]^{1 / 2}
$$

where $\phi$ represents a rather complicated function involving a confluent hypergeometric series. Working curves can be derived from (12.3.23) showing $i_{\mathrm{a}} / i_{\mathrm{c}}$ as a function of $k \tau$ (i.e., $\lambda$ ) and $\left(t_{\mathrm{r}}-\tau\right) / \tau$ (Figure 12.3.14). Similar working curves have been obtained by digital simulation of this case for both chronoamperometry and chronocoulometry (33). These curves can be employed to obtain the rate constant $k$ if a value of $\tau$ can be employed that yields a $\lambda$ in the useful range.

## (c) Chronopotentiometric Methods

The equations governing $\tau$, the $E-t$ curve, and single reversal experiments are given in Section 12.2.2. Cyclic chronopotentiometry shows a continuous decrease in the relative transition times on repeated reversals because of the irreversible loss of R during the course of the experiment (22).

## (d) Other $E_{\mathrm{r}} C$ Mechanisms

The $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}$ case has been treated for a number of variations in addition to the irreversible first-order following reaction discussed here. For example, the case where the product R dimerizes:

$$
2 \mathrm{R} \xrightarrow{k_{2}} \mathrm{R}_{2}
$$

has been treated for several techniques (33-37). This case can be distinguished from the first-order one by the dependence of the electrochemical response on $C_{\mathrm{O}}^{*}$. Also, the varia-

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-499.jpg?height=878&width=704&top_left_y=233&top_left_x=154)
Figure 12.3.14 Working curves for double potential step chronoamperometry for the $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}$ case. $i_{\mathrm{a}}=$ anodic current measured at time $t_{\mathrm{r}} ; i_{c}=$ cathodic current measured at time $t_{\mathrm{f}}=t_{\mathrm{r}}-\tau$; time of potential reversal $=\tau$. [Reprinted with permission from M. Schwarz and I. Shain, J. Phys. Chem., 69, 30 (1965). Copyright 1965, American Chemical Society.]

tion of $i_{\mathrm{p}} / v^{1 / 2}, E_{\mathrm{p}}, \ldots$ with the dimensionless kinetic parameter [which for this secondorder reaction is $\lambda_{2}=k_{2} C_{\mathrm{O}}^{*} t_{1}$ or $\left.\lambda_{2}=k_{2} C_{\mathrm{O}}^{*}(R T / n F v)\right]$ is different. For example, for linear sweep voltammetry in the KP region, the peak potential equation is $(35,37)$

$$
E_{\mathrm{p}}=E_{1 / 2}-\frac{R T}{n F} 0.902+\frac{R T}{3 n F} \ln \left(\frac{2}{3} \lambda_{2}\right)
$$

so that $E_{\mathrm{p}}$ shifts 20 mV (at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) for a tenfold change in scan rate. Other EC schemes, for example, for a reversible following reaction $\left(\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}\right)(25,35)$, or where the product R can react with starting material $\mathrm{O}(33,38)$, have also been discussed.

### 12.3.4 Following Reaction- $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{q}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}$

When the rate of the charge-transfer reaction is sufficiently slow, the observed behavior depends on $k^{0}$ and $\alpha$ [for reaction (12.2.1) considered as a one-electron process] as well as the kinetic parameter $\lambda$ for the following reaction. This case can be important even with fast charge-transfer reactions because, as shown in the discussion of $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}$ reactions, the irreversible following reactions cause the voltammetric wave to shift toward positive values, and this shift away from $E^{0^{\prime}}$ causes a decrease in the rate of the charge-transfer reaction. We consider here only the cyclic voltammetric method $(39,40)$. It is convenient to define a dimensionless parameter $\Lambda$ related to $k^{0}(40)$ :

$$
\Lambda=\frac{k^{0}}{D^{1 / 2} v^{1 / 2}}\left(\frac{R T}{F}\right)^{1 / 2}
$$

and to illustrate the general behavior by a zone diagram (Figure 12.3.15), showing the effect of $\Lambda$ and $\lambda$. The different zones can be explained as follows. For $\lambda<0.1$, the following reaction has no effect and the behavior is characteristic of reversible (DP), quasireversible (QR), or totally irreversible (IR) electron transfers, as described in Chapter 6. Similarly, for large $\Lambda$ values (i.e., the upper portion of Figure 12.3.15), the reaction

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-500.jpg?height=672&width=947&top_left_y=221&top_left_x=139)
Figure 12.3.15 Zone diagram for the one-electron $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{q}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}$ case: $\lambda=(k / v)(R T / F)$; $\Lambda=k^{0}(R T / F)^{1 / 2} /(D v)^{1 / 2}$. [Adapted from L. Nadjo and J.-M. Savéant, J. Electroanal. Chem., 48, 113 (1973), with permission.]

can be considered essentially reversible and the behavior corresponds to that in Section 12.3.3 (regions $\mathrm{DP}, \mathrm{KO}$, and KP ). The effects of joint electron transfer and chemical irreversibility are mainly manifest in zone $\mathrm{KG}(-0.7<\log \Lambda<1.3,-1.2<\log \lambda<0.8)$. Tables with values of the electrochemical parameters as functions of $\Lambda$ and $\lambda$ are given in reference 40 . For example, $E_{\mathrm{p}}$ as a function of $\lambda$ and $\Lambda$ in this region is shown in Figure 12.3.16. Clearly apparent is the change in ( $\partial E_{\mathrm{p}} / \partial \log v$ ) at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ from zero at low $\Lambda$ and $\lambda$ values to $30 / n \mathrm{mV}$ with increasing $\lambda$ and to $59 / n \mathrm{mV}$ with increasing $\Lambda$.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-500.jpg?height=898&width=756&top_left_y=1410&top_left_x=137)
Figure 12.3.16 Variation of $E_{\mathrm{p}}$ for the one-electron $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{q}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}$ case in the intermediate kinetics region ( KG ) as a function of $\log \lambda$, at several values of $\log \Lambda$. Parenthesized numbers indicate limiting slope of each curve. [Data from L. Nadjo and J.-M. Savéant, J. Electroanal. Chem., 48, 113 (1973).]

### 12.3.5 Catalytic Reaction- $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}^{\prime}$

In the catalytic reaction scheme, a species Z , usually nonelectroactive, reacts in the following chemical reaction to regenerate starting material. Thus the problem would involve consideration of a second-order reaction and the diffusion of species Z .

$$
\begin{gathered}
\mathrm{O}+n e \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{R} \\
\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{Z} \xrightarrow{k^{\prime}} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{Y}
\end{gathered}
$$

In most treatments, it is assumed that $Z$ is present in large excess ( $C_{\mathrm{Z}}^{*} \gg C_{\mathrm{O}}^{*}$ ), so that its concentration is essentially unchanged during the voltammetric experiment, and (12.3.28) can be considered a pseudo-first-order reaction. Under these conditions, the kinetic parameter of interest is

$$
\lambda=k^{\prime} C_{\mathrm{Z}}^{*} t \quad \text { or } \quad \lambda=\frac{k^{\prime} C_{\mathrm{Z}}^{*}}{v}\left(\frac{R T}{n F}\right) .
$$

## (a) Linear Sweep and Cyclic Voltammetric Methods

Typical voltammograms for this case, treated in several papers (24, 25), are shown in Figures 12.3.17 and 12.3.18. Note that at sufficiently negative potentials all of the curves tend to a limiting value of current $i_{\infty}$, independent of $v$, given by

$$
i_{\infty}=n F A C_{\mathrm{O}}^{*}\left(D k^{\prime} C_{\mathrm{Z}}^{*}\right)^{1 / 2}
$$

This limiting current arises when the rate of removal of O by the electrolysis is exactly compensated by the rate of production of O by (12.3.28), so that $C_{\mathrm{O}}(x=0)$ attains a value independent of time (or $v$ ). In this KP region, when $\lambda$ becomes large, the $i-E$ curve loses its peak-shaped appearance and becomes a wave. The equation for the wave in this region is

$$
i=\frac{n F A C_{\mathrm{O}}^{*}\left(D k^{\prime} C_{\mathrm{Z}}^{*}\right)^{1 / 2}}{1+\exp \left[\frac{n F}{R T}\left(E-E_{1 / 2}\right)\right]}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-501.jpg?height=647&width=1074&top_left_y=1538&top_left_x=144)
Figure 12.3.17 The $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}^{\prime}$ case: $\mathrm{A}+e \rightleftarrows \mathrm{~B} ; \mathrm{B}+\mathrm{Z} \rightarrow \mathrm{A}+\mathrm{Y}$. Cyclic voltammograms for the system where $E_{\mathrm{A} / \mathrm{B}}^{0^{\prime}}=0 \mathrm{~V}, \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{A}}^{*}=1 \mathrm{~m} M, C_{\mathrm{B}}^{*}=0, C_{\mathrm{Z}}^{*}=1 M, A=1 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}, D_{\mathrm{A}}=D_{\mathrm{B}}=D_{\mathrm{Z}}=10^{-5} \mathrm{cm}^{2} / \mathrm{s}, T=25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, and $k_{\mathrm{f}}=10 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ at scan rates, $v$, of (1) 10 , (2) 1 , (3) 0.1 , and (4) $0.01 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{s}$.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-502.jpg?height=1041&width=891&top_left_y=221&top_left_x=144)
Figure 12.3.18 The $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}^{\prime}$ case: $\mathrm{A}+e \rightleftarrows \mathrm{~B}$; $\mathrm{B}+\mathrm{Z} \rightarrow \mathrm{A}+\mathrm{Y}$. Linear sweep voltammograms for various values of $\lambda=(R T / n F v) k^{\prime} C_{Z}^{*}$ :
(1) $1.00 \times 10^{-2}$;
(2) $1.59 \times 10^{-2}$;
(3) $2.51 \times 10^{-2}$;
(4) $3.98 \times 10^{-2}$;
(5) $6.30 \times 10^{-2}$;
(6) $1.00 \times 10^{-1}$;
(7) $1.59 \times 10^{-1}$;
(8) $2.51 \times 10^{-1}$;
(9) $3.98 \times 10^{-1}$; (10) 1.00 ; (11) $\infty$.
[Reprinted with permission from J.-M. Savéant and E. Vianello, Electrochim. Acta, 10, 905
(1965). Copyright 1965, Pergamon Press PLC.]

or, from (12.3.29) and (12.3.30),

$$
E=E_{1 / 2}+\frac{R T}{n F} \ln \left(\frac{i_{\infty}-i}{i}\right)
$$

Thus in the KP region the analysis of the wave is quite easy and leads immediately to $E_{1 / 2}$ and $k^{\prime}$.

The peak (or plateau) current variation with scan rate changes from a $v^{1 / 2}$ dependence in the DP zone to independence in the KP zone as shown by a plot of $i / i_{d} v s . \lambda$ (Figure 12.3.19). The half-peak potential $E_{\mathrm{p} / 2}$ is independent of $\lambda$ at both high and low values of $\lambda$ and shows a maximum value of $\Delta E_{\mathrm{p} / 2} / \Delta \log v$ in the KI region of about $24 / n \mathrm{mV}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ (Figure 12.3.20).

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-502.jpg?height=485&width=526&top_left_y=1808&top_left_x=147)
Figure 12.3.19 Ratio of kinetic peak current for the $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}^{\prime}$ reaction scheme to diffusion-controlled peak current as a function of $\lambda^{1 / 2}$. [Adapted with permission from R. S. Nicholson and I. Shain, Anal. Chem., 36, 706 (1964). Copyright 1964, American Chemical Society.]

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-503.jpg?height=611&width=773&top_left_y=226&top_left_x=149)
Figure 12.3.20 Variation of half-peak potential with $\lambda$ in the $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}^{\prime}$ case.

For cyclic scans, the ratio of $i_{\mathrm{pa}} / i_{\mathrm{pc}}$ (with $i_{\mathrm{pa}}$ measured from the extension of the cathodic curve) is always unity, independent of $\lambda$, even in the KP region, where on the reverse scan the current tends to retrace the forward scan current (Figure 12.3.17).

A more complicated variation of the $\mathrm{EC}^{\prime}$ scheme, largely studied by voltammetry, is the situation where reaction (12.3.28) is reversible, but the product Y is unstable and undergoes a fast following reaction ( $\mathrm{Y} \rightarrow \mathrm{X}$ ). This instability of Y tends to drive reaction (12.3.28) to the right, so the observed behavior resembles that of the $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}$ scheme. In this case, the $O / R$ couple mediates the reduction of species $Z$, with the ultimate production of species X, and the process is called redox catalysis. By selecting a mediator couple whose $E^{0}$ lies positive of that of the Z/Y couple and noting changes in the cyclic voltammetric response with $v$ and the concentration of Z , one can find the rate constant for the decomposition of Y to X , even if it is too rapid to measure by direct electrochemistry of Z (i.e., as an EC reaction) $(8,9)$.

This approach has been used to study the mechanism of a bond-breaking reaction following electron transfer (a dissociative electron transfer). Consider, for example, the case where species Z is an aryl halide, ArX , that becomes reduced by the electrogenerated reductant to yield the ultimate products Ar and $\mathrm{X}^{-}$. This result can occur either by a concerted path, where bond cleavage occurs simultaneously with electron transfer, or by a stepwise path, where the radical anion, $\mathrm{ArX}^{-}$, is an intermediate. Investigations of such reactions have been carried out by redox catalysis, and theoretical analysis of the structural and thermodynamic factors that affect the reaction path have been described ( 14,41, 42). Similar considerations apply to oxidation reactions, such as of $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-}$ to from two molecules of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$.

## (b) Chronoamperometric Methods

The limiting current for a chronoamperometric experiment $\left[C_{\mathrm{O}}(x=0)=0\right]$ is given by (43-45)

$$
\frac{i}{i_{\mathrm{d}}}=\lambda^{1 / 2}\left[\pi^{1 / 2} \operatorname{erf}\left(\lambda^{1 / 2}\right)+\frac{\exp (-\lambda)}{\lambda^{1 / 2}}\right]
$$

where $i_{\mathrm{d}}$ is the diffusion-controlled current in the absence of the following reaction, (5.2.11). This equation can be used to define the limiting regions of behavior. For small values of $\lambda$ (e.g., $\lambda<0.05$ ), $\operatorname{erf}\left(\lambda^{1 / 2}\right) \approx 2 \lambda^{1 / 2} / \pi^{1 / 2}$ and $i / i_{\mathrm{d}} \approx 1$ (DP region); here the cat-
alytic reaction has no effect. For $\lambda>1.5, \operatorname{erf}\left(\lambda^{1 / 2}\right) \rightarrow 1, \exp (-\lambda) / \lambda^{1 / 2} \rightarrow 0$, and equation (12.3.32) becomes

$$
\frac{i}{i_{\mathrm{d}}}=\pi^{1 / 2} \lambda^{1 / 2}
$$

This defines the pure kinetic region (KP). The chronoamperometric response can be employed to determine $\lambda$ (or $k^{\prime} C_{\mathrm{Z}}^{*}$ ) from a suitable working curve based on (12.3.32) (Figure 12.3.21).

## (c) Chronopotentiometric Methods

The solution to this case (46) yields the following expressions for the concentrations $(t<\tau)$ with $k=k^{\prime} C_{\mathrm{Z}}^{*}$ :

$$
\begin{gathered}
C_{\mathrm{O}}(0, t)=C_{\mathrm{O}}^{*}-\frac{i}{n F A D^{1 / 2} k^{1 / 2}} \operatorname{erf}\left[(k t)^{1 / 2}\right] \\
C_{\mathrm{R}}(0, t)=C_{\mathrm{O}}^{*}-C_{\mathrm{O}}(0, t)
\end{gathered}
$$

At the transition time, $C_{\mathrm{O}}(0, t)=0$ and, from the expression for the transition time $\left(\tau_{\mathrm{d}}\right)$ in the absence of the perturbing reaction, (8.2.14), one obtains

$$
\left(\frac{\tau}{\tau_{\mathrm{d}}}\right)^{1 / 2}=\frac{2 \lambda^{1 / 2}}{\pi^{1 / 2} \operatorname{erf}\left(\lambda^{1 / 2}\right)}
$$

Note that the limiting values of $\tau / \tau_{\mathrm{d}}$ in the DP and KP regions can be obtained by consideration of the behavior at small and large $\lambda$ values (see Problem 12.6). A plot showing this behavior is given in Figure 12.3.22. Note the similarity of the limiting behavior for $\lambda>1.5$, (12.3.36), to the corresponding equation for chronoamperometry, (12.3.33), as well as the similarity of the working curves. The $E-t$ curves can be derived by substitution of the expressions for $C_{\mathrm{O}}(0, t)$ and $C_{\mathrm{R}}(0, t)$ into the Nernst equation (see Problem 12.7).

The equation for the reverse transition time in terms of $\tau_{\mathrm{f}}$ in this case is the same as for the $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}$ case (equation 12.2.32) ( $34,47,48$ ), so that simple reversal experiments cannot distinguish between these cases. However, the variation of $\tau$ with $i$ immediately differentiates between the $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}$ and $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}^{\prime}$ cases.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-504.jpg?height=769&width=873&top_left_y=1584&top_left_x=159)
Figure 12.3.21
Chronoamperometric working curve for the $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}{ }^{\prime}$ case (eq. 12.3.32) for various values of $\lambda^{1 / 2}$, where $\lambda=k^{\prime} C_{\mathrm{Z}}^{*} t$. Dashed line is the KP-region limiting line, (12.3.33).

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-505.jpg?height=836&width=1068&top_left_y=219&top_left_x=149)
Figure 12.3.22 Variation of $\left(\tau / \tau_{\mathrm{d}}\right)^{1 / 2}$ with $\lambda^{1 / 2}$ for $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}^{\prime}$ case in chronopotentiometry $\left(\lambda=k^{\prime} C_{\mathrm{Z}}^{*} \tau\right)$. Dashed line is limiting behavior in the KP region.

### 12.3.6 $\mathbf{E}_{\mathbf{r}} \mathbf{E}_{\mathbf{r}}$ Reactions

We now consider cases in which there are two (or more) heterogeneous electron-transfer reactions.

$$
\begin{array}{ll}
\mathrm{A}+e \rightleftarrows \mathrm{~B} & E_{1}^{0} \\
\mathrm{~B}+e \rightleftarrows \mathrm{C} & E_{2}^{0}
\end{array}
$$

The simplest case is that where both electron-transfer reactions are rapid. Let us consider the cyclic voltammetric behavior for this situation. The appearance of the voltammogram depends upon the location of the standard potentials, $E_{1}^{0}$ and $E_{2}^{0}$, and the spacing between them, $\Delta E^{0}=E_{2}^{0}-E_{1}^{0}$ (Figure 12.3.23) (49).

Typical voltammograms, the variation of the peak current function for the first (cathodic) wave, and the peak splitting between the cathodic and anodic reversal waves
(a)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-505.jpg?height=151&width=642&top_left_y=1786&top_left_x=197)
(b)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-505.jpg?height=153&width=642&top_left_y=1993&top_left_x=197)
(c)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-505.jpg?height=155&width=639&top_left_y=2198&top_left_x=197)
Figure 12.3.23 Different cases for the $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}}$ reaction scheme depending upon relative values of $E_{2}^{0}$ and $E_{1}^{0}$, as expressed by $\Delta E^{0}=E_{2}^{0}-E_{1}^{0}$.

( $\Delta E_{\mathrm{p}}$ ) at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ are shown in Figure 12.3.24. When $\Delta E^{0}>100 \mathrm{mV}$, the second electron step occurs much more easily than the first, and one observes a single wave with characteristics indistinguishable from a single, nernstian, two-electron transfer (i.e., $\Delta E_{\mathrm{p}}=29$ mV , peak current function, $\pi^{1 / 2} \chi(\sigma t) n^{3 / 2}=1.26$ ). As discussed in Sections 3.5 and 12.1, the actual occurrence of a simultaneous transfer of two electrons is, however, very unlikely. As $\Delta E^{0}$ decreases, one observes a single wave with an increasing $\Delta E_{\mathrm{p}}$ until $\Delta E^{0}$ reaches about -80 mV , where a suggestion of two waves can be seen. The two waves become resolvable at $\Delta E^{0}=-125 \mathrm{mV}$, where each wave takes on the characteristics of a one-electron transfer [i.e., $\Delta E_{\mathrm{p}}=58 \mathrm{mV}$, peak current function, $\pi^{1 / 2} \chi(\sigma t) n^{3 / 2}=0.446$ ]. A characteristic of an $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}}$ reaction is the independence of these parameters with scan rate. Under these conditions, the working curves in Figure 12.3.25 can be employed to estimate $\Delta E^{0}$.

It is instructive to consider the chemical and structural factors that affect $\Delta E^{0}$. When the successive electron transfers involve a single molecular orbital, and no large structural changes occur upon electron transfer, then one expects two well-spaced waves ( $\Delta E^{0} \ll$ -125 mV ). For example, the reduction of aromatic hydrocarbons like anthracene occurs with two waves spaced on the order of 500 mV apart (Figure 12.3.26a). However, if transfer occurs to two different groups (two different orbitals) in a molecule (Figure 12.3.26b), then closer spacing between the waves, and even a single wave, can result. Consider a molecule with two identical groups, A, linked in some manner (e.g., with a hydrocarbon chain). When an electron is added to one A group, the energy required to add the second electron depends upon the extent of interaction between the groups. If there is no interaction between them, $\Delta E^{0}=-35.6 \mathrm{mV}$ (at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ), where the curve crosses the dashed line in Figure 12.3.25b. Thus one observes the characteristic splitting of a oneelectron transfer ( $\Delta E_{\mathrm{p}}=58 \mathrm{mV}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ), even though a single wave involving two electron transfers is recorded. Note that a lack of interaction is not represented by $\Delta E^{0}=0$, because statistical (entropic) factors make the second electron transfer slightly more diffi-

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-506.jpg?height=830&width=929&top_left_y=1396&top_left_x=144)
Figure 12.3.24 Changing shapes of cyclic voltammograms for the $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}}$ reaction scheme at different values of $\Delta E^{0}$.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-507.jpg?height=459&width=831&top_left_y=226&top_left_x=151)
Figure 12.3.25 (a) Peak current function [ $\pi^{1 / 2} \chi(\sigma t) n^{3 / 2}$ ] and (b) $\Delta E_{\mathrm{p}}$ vs. $\Delta E^{0}$ for the $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}}$ reaction scheme. The discontinuity in the curve in (b) at negative values of $\Delta E^{0}$ occurs when two waves are resolved.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-507.jpg?height=454&width=826&top_left_y=713&top_left_x=156)
Figure 12.3.25 (a) Peak current function [ $\pi^{1 / 2} \chi(\sigma t) n^{3 / 2}$ ] and (b) $\Delta E_{\mathrm{p}}$ vs. $\Delta E^{0}$ for the $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}}$ reaction scheme. The discontinuity in the curve in (b) at negative values of $\Delta E^{0}$ occurs when two waves are resolved.

cult, in terms of free energy, than the first. It turns out that $\Delta E^{0}=-(2 R T / F) \ln 2(50,51)$. If the groups interact repulsively, then a greater peak splitting is observed. This effect can be seen, for example, in the voltammetry of $\alpha, \omega-9,9^{\prime}$-dianthrylalkanes (Figure 12.3.27). Values of $\Delta E^{0}$ larger than -35.6 mV result when there are attractive interactions, so that the second electron transfer occurs more easily than the first. This almost always requires a major structural rearrangement or large solvation or ion pairing effects occurring as a result of the first electron-transfer step (52).

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-507.jpg?height=623&width=965&top_left_y=1572&top_left_x=149)
Figure 12.3.26 (a) Stepwise addition of electrons to the same molecular orbital in a molecule, R , usually yielding two separated waves. (b) Addition to two separate groups, A , on a molecule, $\mathrm{A} \sim \mathrm{A}$, where the spacing between the waves depends upon the extent of interaction between the groups.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-508.jpg?height=978&width=1018&top_left_y=226&top_left_x=147)
Figure 12.3.27 Cyclic voltammograms for the reduction of $\alpha, \omega-9,9^{\prime}$-dianthrylalkanes (i.e., $\mathrm{An}-\left(\mathrm{CH}_{2}\right)_{n}-\mathrm{An}$, where $\mathrm{An}=$ anthracene) in $1: 1$ benzene:acetonitrile containing 0.1 M tetra- $n$ butylammonium perchlorate at a Pt electrode. As alkane chain length, $n(n=0,2,4,6)$, lengthens, the voltammograms show a decreasing repulsive interaction. [From K. Itaya, A. J. Bard, and M. Szwarc, Z. Physik. Chem. N. F., 112, 1 (1978), with permission.]

Whenever an $\mathrm{E}_{r} \mathrm{E}_{r}$ reaction takes place, one must consider the possibility of a dispro-portionation-comproportionation equilibrium,

$$
2 \mathrm{~B} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{~A}+\mathrm{C} \quad K_{\text {disp }}=k_{\mathrm{f}} / k_{\mathrm{b}}=[\mathrm{A}][\mathrm{C}] /[\mathrm{B}]^{2}
$$

also developing in the solution near the electrode. The extent of the reaction, as measured by the equilibrium constant, $K_{\mathrm{disp}}$, is governed by $\Delta E^{0}$ :

$$
(R T / F) \ln K_{\text {disp }}=\Delta E^{0}=E_{2}^{0}-E_{1}^{0}
$$

For example, for two well-separated waves ( $\Delta E^{0}<0$ ), $K_{\text {disp }}$ is small, and reaction (12.3.39) lies to the left (i.e., the comproportionation of A and C dominates the disproportionation of B ). Thus, at potentials of the second wave, C diffusing away from the electrode can reduce $A$ diffusing towards it, so that the concentration profiles of $A, B$, and $C$ are perturbed from those that would exist if the solution phase reaction did not occur. However, for the $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}}$ reaction scheme, the observed voltammogram is independent of the rates of the forward and back reactions in (12.3.39), because, at any given potential, the average oxidation state in any layer of solution near the electrode remains the same (53). At potentials of the second wave, species A, which would take two electrons, is removed by the comproportionation reaction, but two B molecules are produced, and each of these would take one electron for no net change. This is not true, however, if the heterogeneous rate constants for the electron transfer are slow (Section 12.3.7) or in ECE reactions (Section 12.3.8).

The same considerations apply for reactions involving more than two electron transfers, that is, $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \ldots$ schemes. The observed behavior can vary from a set of $n$ resolved
one-electron waves to a single $n$-electron wave. For example, a solution of $\mathrm{C}_{60}$ shows up to 6 separated one-electron waves, attributed to the addition of electrons to three degenerate orbitals in the molecule. However, for solutions of many polymers, such as poly(vinylferrocene) (PVF), only a single wave is observed, with a $\Delta E_{\mathrm{p}}$ characteristic of a one-electron process and a peak height governed by the degree of polymerization and the total number of electrons added per molecule (51). This behavior is consistent with the lack of interaction among ferrocene centers on a polymer chain. For example, the oxidation of PVF containing (on the average) 74 ferrocene units per molecule produces a 74 electron wave whose shape is essentially that of a nernstian one-electron-transfer reaction.

### 12.3.7 $\mathbf{E}_{\mathbf{q}} \mathbf{E}_{\mathbf{q}}$ Reactions

The treatment of EE reactions becomes more complex when one or both of the electrontransfer reactions are quasireversible. Even in the simplest case, where $n_{1}=n_{2}=1$ and $\alpha_{1}=\alpha_{2}=0.5$, the cyclic voltammetric behavior depends upon three parameters, $\Delta E^{0}, k_{1}^{0}$, and $k_{2}^{0}$ (rather than the single parameter $\Delta E^{0}$ for the $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}}$ scheme in Section 12.3.6). These three parameters can be represented in different ways, for example, in terms of dimensionless aggregates like $\Lambda_{1}=k_{1}^{0} /[D v(F / R T)]^{1 / 2}$ and $k_{1}^{0} / k_{2}^{0}$.

Consider the special case of an $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{q}}$ reaction, where the first electron transfer is fast (nernstian) and the second somewhat slower. The cyclic voltammetric behavior, such as for $\Delta E^{0}=0$ as shown in Figure 12.3.28, depends upon the scan rate. At smaller scan rates (curve $A$ ), a single wave with behavior approaching the $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}}$ case, is seen. As the scan rate is increased (i.e., as $\Lambda_{2}$ decreases), the first electron-transfer wave remains reversible and centered on $E^{0}$, but the slower second electron transfer results in a

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-509.jpg?height=946&width=1221&top_left_y=1258&top_left_x=125)
Figure 12.3.28 Representative behavior for an $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{q}}$ reaction. System with $\Delta E^{0}=0, E_{1}^{0}=0$, $n_{1}=n_{2}=1, \alpha_{1}=\alpha_{2}=0.5, k_{1}^{0}=10^{4} \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}, k_{2}^{0}=10^{-2} \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}, D=10^{-5} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} / \mathrm{s}, C=1 \mathrm{~m} M$, $A=1 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}, T=25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, and scan rates, $v$, of (a) 1 ; (b) 10 ; (c) 100 ; (d) $1000 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{s}$. Rate constants for (12.3.39) are assumed to be zero. Current in amperes; potential in mV .

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-510.jpg?height=930&width=1201&top_left_y=221&top_left_x=135)
Figure 12.3.29 Representative behavior for an $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{q}}$ reaction. System with all parameters as in Figure 12.3.28, except $\Delta E^{0}=150 \mathrm{mV}$. Scan rates of (a) 1; (b) 10; (c) 100 ; (d) $1000 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{s}$. Rate constants for (12.3.39) are assumed to be zero.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-510.jpg?height=975&width=1209&top_left_y=1261&top_left_x=135)
Figure 12.3.30 Representative behavior for an $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{q}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}}$ reaction. System with $\Delta E^{0}=0, n_{1}=n_{2}=1 \alpha_{1}=\alpha_{2}=0.5, k_{1}^{0}=10^{-2} \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}, k_{2}^{0}=10^{4} \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}, D=10^{-5} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} / \mathrm{s}, C=1 \mathrm{~m} M, A=1 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}, T=25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and scan rates, $v$, of (a) 1; (b) 10; (c) 100; (d) $1000 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{s}$. Current in amperes; potential in mV .

second wave splitting away from the first (curves $C$ and $D$ ), with larger splitting found at higher scan rates. The situation is somewhat different for an $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{q}}$ reaction where the second electron transfer occurs more easily than the first, for example, for $\Delta E^{0}=150$ mV (Figure 12.3.29). In this case, when species B is formed, it is readily reduced to C , because the reaction is occurring at potentials well negative of $E_{2}^{0}$. Thus at high scan rates (curves $C$ and $D$ ) the cathodic wave is not split and reversibility is not seen at the potential of the first wave.

The $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{q}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}}$ reaction is another special case, where the first electron transfer is now the rate-determining step. The general trend in this case (Figure 12.3.30) is a shift in $E_{\mathrm{pc}}$ to more negative values with increasing scan rate, without splitting of the cathodic wave. The anodic wave splits at higher scan rates because the oxidation of species B to A occurs at more positive potentials.

A general treatment of the $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{q}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{q}}$ scheme is probably best carried out through digital simulations, although attempts have been made to give working curves, such as of $\Delta E_{\mathrm{p}}$ or $i_{\mathrm{pa}} / i_{\mathrm{pc}}$ vs. $\Lambda_{1}$ at different values of $\Delta E^{0}$ and $k_{1}^{0} / k_{2}^{0}=\Lambda_{1} / \Lambda_{2}$ (54). The situation is made even more complicated, however, by the fact that the observed voltammogram is affected (unlike the $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}}$ case) by a disproportionation-comproportionation equilibrium, (12.3.39), so yet another variable (the rate constant for the homogeneous disproportionation reaction) must be considered. The effect of this rate constant for an $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{q}}$ reaction is shown in Figure 12.3.31. For the conditions given in this example, the disproportionation has little

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-511.jpg?height=1259&width=650&top_left_y=1092&top_left_x=156)
Figure 12.3.31 Representative behavior for the $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{q}}$ process shown in Figure 12.3.28D including a disproportionation reaction. $\Delta E^{0}=0, E_{1}^{0}=0, n_{1}=n_{2}=1, \alpha_{1}=\alpha_{2}= 0.5, k_{1}^{0}=10^{4} \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}, k_{2}^{0}=10^{-2} \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$, $D=10^{-5} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} / \mathrm{s}, C=1 \mathrm{~m} M, A=1 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$, $T=25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, and $v=1000 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{s}$. Disproportionation rate constant of $(a) 10^{10}$; (b) $10^{8}$; (c) $10^{6} \mathrm{M}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. Current in amperes; potential in mV .

effect as long as its rate constant is less than about $10^{-6} M^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. However, large changes are noted for larger values of the rate constant, since this solution phase reaction can assist in equilibrating the reactant species at the electrode surface, thus causing the behavior to appear more similar to that found at smaller scan rates.

### 12.3.8 ECE Reactions

The general ECE reaction scheme is ${ }^{2}$

$$
\begin{array}{cc}
\mathrm{A}+e \rightleftarrows \mathrm{~B} & E_{1}^{0} \\
\mathrm{~B} \stackrel{k_{\mathrm{b}}}{\rightleftarrows} \mathrm{C} & K=k_{\mathrm{f}} / k_{\mathrm{b}} \\
\mathrm{C}+e \rightleftarrows \mathrm{D} & E_{2}^{0} \\
\mathrm{~B}+\mathrm{C} \stackrel{k_{\mathrm{f}}}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{~A}+\mathrm{D} &
\end{array}
$$

In the case of greatest interest, $E_{2}^{0} \gg E_{1}^{0}$, so that species C is much easier to reduce than species A . Typically, $\Delta E^{0}=E_{2}^{0}-E_{1}^{0} \geq 180 \mathrm{mV}$. In the opposite limiting case, when the second reaction occurs at significantly more negative potentials than the first ( $\Delta E^{0} \leq$ -180 mV ), sequential stepwise addition of electrons results in two voltammetric waves. The first wave is based on an EC sequence that can be analyzed as described in Section 12.3.3, without consideration of the second electron-transfer step.

Reaction (12.3.44) is included in the scheme because species B is capable of reducing species C in a homogeneous reaction near the electrode surface. With $\Delta E^{0} \geq 180 \mathrm{mV}$, (12.3.44) can be taken as irreversible to the right. Because species $B$ and $C$ are at the same oxidation level, this reaction can be considered to be a disproportionation reaction, and ECE-schemes that include it are denoted ECE/DISP mechanisms.

## (a) Linear Scan and Cyclic Voltammetric Methods

For the ECE case under consideration here, only a single wave (wave I, reduction of A and C ) is observed on the first (cathodic) scan (Figure 12.3.32). This wave occurs near $E_{1}^{0}$, since any C formed at this potential is immediately reduced to D . On the reverse scan, a reversal wave (II) is observed for oxidation of $B$ if reaction (12.3.42) is not too rapid (Figure 12.3.32b). As the reverse scan continues, a second reversal wave (III) is seen representing oxidation of D to C . Another reversal of the scan reveals a corresponding cathodic wave (IV) for the reduction of C to D . The relative sizes of waves II, III, and IV depend upon the magnitudes of the rate constants in the reaction scheme, $k_{\mathrm{f}}, k_{\mathrm{b}}$, and $k_{\mathrm{d}}$. The curves shown in Figure 12.3.32 are for an $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}}$ sequence where $k_{\mathrm{f}}=k_{\mathrm{d}}=0$ and the dimensionless parameter of interest is $\lambda=(R T / F v) k_{\mathrm{b}}$. A zone diagram for this case can be given in terms of the normalized peak current of wave $\mathrm{I}, n_{\text {app }}$, as a function of $\lambda$ (Figure 12.3.33), where

$$
n_{\mathrm{app}}=i_{\mathrm{p}} / F A C_{\mathrm{A}}^{*}(\pi D \sigma)^{1 / 2} \chi(\sigma t)=i_{\mathrm{p}}(\mathrm{I}) /\left[i_{\mathrm{p}}(\mathrm{I})(\lambda=0)\right]
$$

When $\lambda$ is small (region DP), B is stable, and $n_{\text {app }} \approx 1$. In this region, waves III and IV are very small. For large $\lambda$ (region KP), essentially all B is converted to C which immediately reacts to form D; therefore $n_{\text {app }} \approx 2$, and waves III and IV are prominent. The quantitative treatment of this scheme has been described $(55,56)$.

[^104]![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-513.jpg?height=998&width=1209&top_left_y=211&top_left_x=130)
Figure 12.3.32 Cyclic voltammograms for the $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}}$ case obtained by digital simulation for $E_{1}^{0}=-0.44 \mathrm{~V}, E_{2}^{0}=-0.20 \mathrm{~V}$ for different values of $\lambda=\left(k_{\mathrm{b}} / v\right)(R T / F) ; n_{1}=n_{2}=1 .(a) \lambda=0$ (unperturbed nernstian reaction); (b) $\lambda=0.05$; (c) $\lambda=0.40$; (d) $\lambda=2$.

When reaction 12.3.42 is reversible, the overall process is denoted as $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}}$, and the observed voltammetric behavior depends upon both $K$ and $\lambda=(R T / F v)\left(k_{\mathrm{f}}+k_{\mathrm{b}}\right)$. The zone diagram for this case is shown in Figure 12.3.34 (57, 58). When $K$ is large, species B is always favored and a simple one-electron process is observed (region DO). At the opposite limit, when $K$ is such that conversion of B to C takes place and $\lambda$ is large, nernstian behavior is again found, but with a two-electron wave seen for the overall reaction

$$
\mathrm{A}+2 e \rightleftarrows \mathrm{D}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-513.jpg?height=514&width=885&top_left_y=1786&top_left_x=149)
Figure 12.3.33 Variation of $n_{\text {app }}$ and $i_{\mathrm{pa}}(\mathrm{II}) / i_{\mathrm{pc}}(\mathrm{I})$ with $\lambda$ in cyclic voltammetry for the $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}}$ system shown in Figure 12.3.32. $n_{\text {app }}$ defined in (12.3.45). Zones showing limiting behavior also indicated.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-514.jpg?height=517&width=768&top_left_y=226&top_left_x=154)
Figure 12.3.34 Zone diagram for the $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}}$ mechanism, where $K=k_{\mathrm{f}} / k_{\mathrm{b}}=C_{\mathrm{B}} / C_{\mathrm{C}}$ and $\lambda= (R T / F v)\left(k_{\mathrm{f}}+k_{\mathrm{b}}\right)$. [Reprinted from J.-M. Savéant, C. P. Andrieux, and L. Nadjo, J. Electroanal. Chem., 41, 137 (1973), with permission from Elsevier Science.]

(region DE). By combining the relevant thermodynamic data for this set of reactions, one finds that the standard potential of $(12.3 .46), E^{0 *}$, is given by

$$
E^{0 *}=\left(E_{1}^{0}+E_{2}^{0}\right) / 2-(R T / 2 n F) \ln K
$$

The observed wave in the DE region occurs at this potential. For smaller values of $\lambda$, one traverses different regions where kinetics control the observed behavior. The special case of the $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}}$ sequence can be seen in this diagram where $K$ is very small (e.g., $\log K=-4$ ).

In general, one must also take account of the disproportionation reaction, (12.3.44). Limiting cases where this reaction dominates for the conversion of A to D [i.e., where the second electron-transfer reaction at the electrode, (12.3.43), does not occur] are denoted as DISP schemes $(12,59)$. Although (12.3.43) is thermodynamically favored, it might not occur to a significant extent because species C does not reach the electrode. This happens, for example, when $k_{\mathrm{b}}$ is small, so that C is produced some distance from the electrode, and $k_{\mathrm{d}}$ is large, so that C diffusing towards the electrode mainly reacts with B diffusing away from it.

These DISP schemes can be divided into two subcases: DISP1, where (12.3.42) is the rate-determining-step; and DISP2, where (12.3.44) is the rate-determining-step and (12.3.42) is at equilibrium. A simplified zone diagram can be drawn (neglecting the intermediate regions) in terms of the parameters $K$ and $p=k_{\mathrm{d}} C_{\mathrm{A}}^{*} /\left[\left(k_{\mathrm{f}}+k_{\mathrm{b}}\right)^{1 / 2}(R T / F v)^{1 / 2}\right]$, where the different limiting cases apply (Figure 12.3.35) (7). A more complete diagram is

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-514.jpg?height=734&width=797&top_left_y=1615&top_left_x=156)
Figure 12.3.35 Simplified zone diagram showing where different limiting ECE and DISP cases apply in terms of the parameters $K$ and $p=k_{\mathrm{d}} C_{\mathrm{A}}^{*} /\left[\left(k_{\mathrm{f}}+\right.\right. \left.k_{\mathrm{b}}\right)^{3 / 2}(R T / F v)^{1 / 2}$ ]. [From C. P. Andrieux and J.-M. Savéant, in "Investigation of Rates and Mechanisms of Reactions," Part II, 4th ed., C. F. Bernasconi, Ed., Wiley-Interscience, New York, 1986, with permission.]

shown in the following section covering chronoamperometric methods. Clearly the general behavior of this system is complicated, and collection of voltammetric data over a wide range of the experimental variables $C_{\mathrm{A}}^{*}$ and $v$ is required to confirm the mechanism and extract the kinetic parameters ( $k_{\mathrm{d}}, k_{\mathrm{f}}$, and $k_{\mathrm{b}}$ ). Note also that the above treatment assumed a rather large $\Delta E^{0}$ value. For $0 \leq \Delta E^{0}<180 \mathrm{mV}$, the waves for the two halfreactions will begin to overlap. Theoretical voltammograms are usually obtained by digital simulation for different values of $\Delta E^{0}, k_{\mathrm{d}}, k_{\mathrm{f}}, k_{\mathrm{b}}, C_{\mathrm{A}}^{*}$, and $v$.

This system becomes even more complex when the heterogeneous kinetics of the electron-transfer reactions are taken into account. While a theoretical treatment has been given with either one or both of these reactions assumed to be totally irreversible (55), such complications are rarely dealt with in practice.

## (b) Chronoamperometric Methods

There are two limiting cases for this scheme, assuming a step to potentials where $C_{\mathrm{A}}(x=0)=C_{\mathrm{C}}(x=0)=0$. For $\lambda \rightarrow 0$, the behavior approaches the simple unperturbed reaction involving only (12.3.41), that is, equation (5.2.11) with $n=1$ (zone DP) (Figure 12.3.33, with $\lambda=k_{\mathrm{b}} t$ ). For $\lambda \rightarrow \infty$, the behavior again approaches diffusion control with both electron transfers occurring, that is, equation (5.2.11) with $n=2$, denoted $\left(i t^{1 / 2}\right)_{\infty}$ (zone KP). For intermediate values of $\lambda$, the following equation applies (60):

$$
\left(i t^{1 / 2}\right) /\left(i t^{1 / 2}\right)_{\infty}=1-\left(e^{-\lambda / 2}\right)
$$

Thus, zone DP is attained when $\lambda<0.05$, while KP results when $\lambda>3$. An alternative expression for this behavior is

$$
n_{\text {app }}=2-e^{-\lambda}
$$

A general treatment of the ECE-DISP system for chronoamperometry has also appeared (59) with zone diagrams (including some three-dimensional ones) to describe the behavior under various conditions. For example, the general case for pure kinetic conditions is shown in Figure 12.3.36.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-515.jpg?height=922&width=814&top_left_y=1427&top_left_x=139)
Figure 12.3.36 Zone diagram for the ECE-DISP scheme for chronoamperometry in terms of $K, \lambda_{\mathrm{d}}=k_{\mathrm{d}} t$, and $\lambda=\left(k_{\mathrm{f}}+k_{\mathrm{b}}\right) t$. This diagram also applies to voltammetry when the characteristic time in the $\lambda$ expressions, $t$, is replaced by ( $R T / F v$ ). Compare with the simplified diagram in Figure 12.3.35. [Reprinted from C. Amatore and J.-M. Savéant, J. Electroanal. Chem., 102, 21 (1979), with permission from Elsevier Science.]

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-516.jpg?height=623&width=758&top_left_y=224&top_left_x=200)
Figure 12.3.37 Variation of $\tau_{\mathrm{r}} / \tau_{\mathrm{f}}$ with $\lambda$ for chronopotentiometry in the $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}}$ reaction with $n_{1}=n_{2}$. [Data from H. B. Herman and A. J. Bard, J. Phys. Chem., 70, 396 (1966).]

While the ECE reaction can be studied by dc polarography (61-63), the limited time window in this method greatly restricts its utility.

## (c) Chronopotentiometric Methods

For constant current experiments on $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}}$ systems with $\Delta E^{0}>0$, the results are analogous to those found in chronoamperometry and linear scan voltammetry (10, 64). For small $\lambda$ in zone DP , as $i \rightarrow \infty$, (8.2.14) applies with $n=1$. In zone KP with large $\lambda$ as $i \rightarrow$ 0 , (8.2.14) again holds with $n=2$. The overall dependence of $i \tau^{1 / 2}$ on $\lambda$ involves a rather complicated expression; the general trend is similar to that in Figure 12.3.33. On current reversal, only B is oxidized near the forward wave, and the ratio of transition times $\tau_{\mathrm{r}} / \tau_{\mathrm{f}}$ as a function of $\lambda$ is shown in Figure 12.3.37.

### 12.3.9 Other Reaction Schemes

It is beyond the scope of this work to consider the many other reaction schemes (e.g., $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \mathrm{C} \overleftarrow{\mathrm{E}}$, electron-transfer catalyzed reactions, square schemes) that have been treated theoretically and applied to actual systems. Details of the appropriate equations and procedures to treat these cases, as well as references to the original literature, can be found in reviews (7-9, 14, 65-68). Many applications of electrochemical techniques to the elucidation of organic $(69,70)$ and inorganic $(71,72)$ reaction mechanisms have appeared.

### 12.4 ROTATING DISK AND RING-DISK METHODS

The rotating disk electrode (RDE) discussed in Chapter 9 has proven to be very useful in studies of coupled homogeneous reactions. The information content of the experimental results is high, since the coupled reactions cause perturbations in the limiting current, the half-wave potential, and the ring current at the RRDE; and an intuitive appreciation of the interpretation of the $i-\bar{E}$ curves is easy to attain. Also, by working at potentials in the limiting current region, complications caused by slow heterogeneous electron-transfer steps can be avoided. Although rigorous theoretical treatments at the RDE, and especially the RRDE, are rather difficult, a number of reaction schemes have been investigated, and the successful application of digital simulation methods has allowed even complex reaction schemes to be treated.

An important advantage of the RDE is that measurements are made under steadystate (i.e., time-independent) conditions. Thus, the general approaches described in Sections 1.4.2 and 1.5 can be applied, with the appropriate choices of $m$ (the masstransfer coefficient) and $\mu$ (the reaction layer thickness). Moreover, equations of the same form are found with other steady-state techniques and can be found by replacing $m=0.62 D^{2 / 3} \omega^{1 / 2} \nu^{-1 / 6}$ for the RDE with $m$ for the particular technique. For example, $m=D / r_{0}$ at hemispherical UMEs at long times (or slow sweep rates).

### 12.4.1 Theoretical Treatments

As with LSV or CV, the mass-transfer equations for the various species must be changed to take account of loss or production of material because of the coupled reactions. Thus for the CE mechanism [(12.3.1) and (12.3.2)] at the RDE , (9.3.13) becomes

$$
v_{y}\left(\frac{\partial C_{\mathrm{O}}}{\partial y}\right)=D_{\mathrm{O}}\left(\frac{\partial^{2} C_{\mathrm{O}}}{\partial y^{2}}\right)+k_{\mathrm{f}} C_{\mathrm{Y}}-k_{\mathrm{b}} C_{\mathrm{O}}
$$

This must be solved with the appropriate boundary conditions. For investigations at the RRDE, the radial convective terms must be included as well. Thus, in the treatment of the $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}$ scheme at the RRDE [(12.2.1) and (12.2.2)], where product R is oxidized at the ring electrode, the appropriate equation for R is

$$
v_{y}\left(\frac{\partial C_{\mathrm{R}}}{\partial y}\right)+v_{r}\left(\frac{\partial C_{\mathrm{R}}}{\partial r}\right)=D_{\mathrm{R}}\left(\frac{\partial^{2} C_{\mathrm{R}}}{\partial y^{2}}\right)-k C_{\mathrm{R}}
$$

The solution of this equation would then follow as described in Section 9.4. The modifications of the mass-transfer equations for the different cases generally follow those used in voltammetric methods as shown in Table 12.2.1. Appropriate dimensionless parameters are listed in Table 12.3.1. It is usually not possible to solve these equations analytically, so various approximations (e.g., the reaction layer approach, as described in Section 1.5.2), digital simulations, or other numerical methods must be employed. The behavior of systems at the RDE can be analyzed by means of the zone diagrams employed for voltammetry (Section 12.3) by redefining the parameter $\lambda$. This is accomplished by replacing ( $R T / n F v$ ) with the term $\delta^{2} / D\left[=(1.61)^{2} \nu^{1 / 3} / \omega D^{1 / 3}\right]$ (7).

### 12.4.2 Preceding Reaction- $\mathbf{C}_{\mathbf{r}} \mathbf{E}_{\mathbf{r}}$

The solution for the limiting current at the RDE for the reaction scheme in (12.3.1)-(12.3.3) has been obtained (73). To solve the convective-diffusion equations it was assumed that the reactions were rapid, so that $(D / \nu)^{1 / 3} \ll\left(k_{\mathrm{f}}+k_{\mathrm{b}}\right) / \omega$. With this condition, the limiting current is given by

$$
i=\frac{n F A D C^{*}}{1.61 D^{1 / 3} \omega^{-1 / 2} \nu^{1 / 6}+D^{1 / 2} / K k^{1 / 2}}
$$

where $k=k_{\mathrm{f}}+k_{\mathrm{b}}$ and $C^{*}=C_{\mathrm{O}}^{*}+C_{\mathrm{Y}}^{*}$. The first term in the denominator is $\delta$, the Nernst diffusion layer thickness:

$$
\delta=1.61 D^{1 / 3} \omega^{-1 / 2} \nu^{1 / 6}=D / m
$$

and the second term, which also has dimensions of length, contains the so-called reaction layer thickness $\mu$

$$
\mu=(D / k)^{1 / 2}
$$

Parameters for the reaction can be determined from the variation of $i$ with $\omega$. Note that at small $\omega$, the first term of the denominator predominates ( $\delta \gg \mu / K$ ), and the observed current is the mass-transfer-controlled limiting current $i_{l}$ (equation 9.3.22). At high $\omega(\delta \ll \mu / K)$, the pure kinetic current results:

$$
i=n F A D^{1 / 2} C^{*} K k^{1 / 2}
$$

The current under these conditions is independent of $\omega$ and is identical to that found for linear scan voltammetry and chronoamperometry, (12.3.6). Equation 12.4.3 can also be written in the form (74)

$$
\frac{i}{\omega^{1 / 2}}=\frac{i_{l}}{\omega^{1 / 2}}-i\left(\frac{D^{1 / 6}}{1.61 \nu^{1 / 6} K k^{1 / 2}}\right)
$$

so that a plot of $i / \omega^{1 / 2}$ vs. $i$ can be used to obtain $K\left(k_{\mathrm{f}}+k_{\mathrm{b}}\right)^{1 / 2}$. This case has also been treated for the rotating ring electrode (75).

### 12.4.3 Following Reaction- $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}$

A steady-state treatment of this case using the reaction layer concept was given in Section 1.5.2. The limiting cathodic current is not affected by the following reaction, but the curve is shifted toward positive potentials because of the following reaction, with $E_{1 / 2}$ becoming a function of $\omega$. The theoretical equation based on a more exact treatment of this case is (76)

$$
E_{1 / 2}=E^{0^{\prime}}+\left(\frac{R T}{n F}\right) \ln \left(\frac{\delta}{\mu}\right) \operatorname{coth}\left(\frac{\delta}{\mu}\right)
$$

where $\delta / \mu=1.61 k^{1 / 2} \nu^{1 / 6} D^{-1 / 6} \omega^{-1 / 2}$. When $\delta / \mu$ becomes large (i.e., for $k / \omega>100$ ), $\operatorname{coth}(\delta / \mu) \rightarrow 1$, and (12.4.8) becomes

$$
E_{1 / 2}=E^{0^{\prime}}+\left(\frac{R T}{n F}\right) \ln \left(1.61 D^{-1 / 6} \nu^{1 / 6}\right)+\frac{R T}{2 n F} \ln \left(\frac{k}{\omega}\right)
$$

Note that the equation from the approximate treatment, (1.5.25), becomes the same as (12.4.9) by choosing $\mu=(D / k)^{1 / 2}$ (the same reaction layer thickness defined in Section 12.4.2). The limits of applicability of these equations have been examined (76, 77). Note also the similarity between (12.4.9) and the voltammetric equation (12.3.22). (Recall that $\lambda=k / \omega$ for the RDE.)

The RRDE is especially useful in the study of EC reactions. The ring is set at a potential where the mass-transfer-controlled oxidation of R back to O [the reverse of (12.2.1)] occurs, and one evaluates $k$ from the difference between the measured (kinetic) collection efficiency $N_{\mathrm{K}}\left(=-i_{\mathrm{r}} / i_{\mathrm{d}}\right)$ and the value $N$ found in the absence of the perturbing reaction (see Section 9.4.2). An approximate treatment of steady-state kinetic collection efficiencies for thin-ring/thin-gap electrodes, later extended to a wider range of electrode geometries, has been presented (78-80). For such electrodes two approximate equations were suggested (78): For $4.5<\kappa<3.5$

$$
N_{\mathrm{K}} \approx 1.75 \kappa^{-3} \exp \left[-4 \kappa^{3}\left(r_{2}-r_{1}\right) / r_{1}\right]
$$

For $\kappa<0.5$

$$
\frac{N}{N_{\mathrm{K}}}=1+1.28\left(\frac{\nu}{D}\right)^{1 / 3}\left(\frac{k}{\omega}\right)
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-519.jpg?height=575&width=700&top_left_y=224&top_left_x=147)
Figure 12.4.1 Collection efficiency $v s$. XKT $\left[=\kappa^{2}=k \nu^{1 / 3} / \omega D^{1 / 3}(0.51)^{2 / 3}\right]$. Curve obtained from digital simulation of an $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}$ reaction at thin-ring/thin-gap electrode ( $r_{2} / r_{1}=1.02, r_{3} / r_{2}=1.02$ ). Points show $N_{\mathrm{K}}$ calculated from (12.4.11). [From K. B. Prater and A. J, Bard, J. Electrochem. Soc., 117, 335 (1970), reprinted by permission of the publisher, The Electrochemical Society, Inc.]

where

$$
\kappa=k^{1 / 2} \omega^{-1 / 2} D^{-1 / 6} \nu^{1 / 6}(0.51)^{-1 / 3}
$$

and $r_{1}$ and $r_{2}$ are the radii of the disk and inner edge of the ring, respectively (see Figure 9.4.1). These expressions hold, however, only for electrodes where $r_{2} / r_{1} \approx r_{3} / r_{1}$, which are very difficult to construct in practice. An alternate, more complicated expression for $N_{\mathrm{K}}$ was therefore suggested $(79,80)$ :

$$
N_{K}=N-\left(\beta^{\prime}\right)^{2 / 3}\left(1-U_{*} A_{1}^{-1}\right)+\frac{1}{2} A_{1}^{-1} A_{2}^{2} \kappa^{2} U_{*}\left(\beta^{\prime}\right)^{4 / 3}-2 A_{2} \kappa^{2} T_{2}
$$

where $A_{1}=1.288, A_{2}=0.643 \nu^{1 / 6} D^{1 / 3}, \beta^{\prime}=3 \ln \left(r_{3} / r_{2}\right), U_{*}=\kappa^{-1} \tanh \left(A_{1} \kappa\right)$, and $T_{2}$ is a rather complicated small factor approximately equal to $0.718 \ln \left(r_{2} / r_{1}\right)$.

The problem of the $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}$ reaction has also been attacked using digital simulation methods (81) (Appendix B). In this approach, no approximations need be made about electrode geometry. However, a separate simulation is required for each electrode geometry and each value of $\kappa$. Results from typical simulations of $N_{\mathrm{K}}$ as a function of the simulation parameter XKT ( $=\kappa^{2}$ ) are shown in Figures 12.4.1 and 12.4.2, which also contain predictions from the approximate equations in the appropriate regions. The range of rate constants that can be measured by the RRDE technique generally is, with rather generous limits, $0.3<\kappa<5$ (80). As can be seen in Figure 12.4.2 when $\kappa<0.3, N_{\mathrm{K}} \approx N$; while for $\kappa>5, N_{\mathrm{K}} \rightarrow 0$ and the ring current becomes too small to measure. For the usual range of $\omega, D$, and $\nu$, the range of measurable rate constants is approximately $0.03<k<10^{3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-519.jpg?height=549&width=814&top_left_y=1757&top_left_x=149)
Figure 12.4.2 $N_{\mathrm{K}}$ vs. XKT as in Figure 12.4.1: Points show calculations from (12.4.10). [From K. B. Prater and A. J. Bard, J. Electrochem. Soc., 117, 335 (1970), reprinted by permission of the publisher, The Electrochemical Society, Inc.]

Theoretical treatments are also available for $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}$ schemes involving second-order reactions (81, 82), as well as for the disk and ring transients in this reaction scheme (81).

### 12.4.4 Catalytic Reaction- $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}^{\prime}$

A solution based on the reaction layer approach for this mechanism [see (12.3.27) and (12.3.28)] follows closely that given for the $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}}$ reaction discussed in Section 12.4.2 (73). Under the conditions that $\delta \gg \mu$ (i.e., that $\omega(D / \nu)^{1 / 3} \ll 3 k^{\prime} C_{\mathrm{Z}}^{*}$ ), a limiting kinetic current, independent of $\omega$, is found:

$$
i=n F A D^{1 / 2} C_{\mathrm{O}}^{*}\left(k^{\prime} C_{\mathrm{Z}}^{*}\right)^{1 / 2}
$$

Note that this equation is the same as (12.3.29). This limiting current holds only in the region of small $\omega$. When $\lambda\left(=k^{\prime} C_{\mathrm{Z}}^{*} / \omega\right)$ becomes small, the behavior approaches the mass-transfercontrolled limiting current. Results of a digital simulation of the catalytic case (83) are shown in Figure 12.4.3. Other treatments of the $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}^{\prime}$ case at the RDE, as well as variations of this mechanism, have also appeared (84-86). The treatment of the $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}^{\prime}$ case for the RRDE by digital simulation techniques showed that the results (i.e., plots of $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{K}}$ vs. XKT) are indistinguishable from those of the $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}$ case for first- or pseudo-first-order reactions (83).

### 12.4.5 ECE Reactions

The behavior of systems at the RDE undergoing the general ECE/DISP scheme (Section 12.3.8) can be analyzed by means of the zone diagrams employed for voltammetry with the appropriate expressions for $\lambda$ and $\lambda_{\mathrm{d}}$. Most RDE treatments, however, have been concerned with the $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}}$ sequence, which has been treated at several different levels of approximation. Karp (87) proposed the following equation for the limiting current:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& i=i_{l}\left[2-\frac{\tanh (\delta / \mu)}{\delta / \mu}\right] \\
& \frac{n_{\text {app }}}{n}=2-\frac{\tanh (\delta / \mu)}{\delta / \mu}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $n_{1}=n_{2}=n, i_{l}$ is the $n$-electron mass-transfer-limiting current given in (9.3.22), and $\delta$ and $\mu$ are as defined in (12.4.4) and (12.4.5). Filinovskii (88) proposed the equation

$$
i=0.94 i_{l}\left[2-\frac{\left(1+\delta^{2} / 1.9 \mu^{2}\right)^{1 / 2}}{1+\delta^{2} / \mu^{2}}\right]
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-520.jpg?height=481&width=573&top_left_y=1845&top_left_x=224)
Figure 12.4.3 Simulated disk current for the catalytic reaction $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}^{\prime}$. Curve a: In absence of a following reaction. Curve $b$ : In the presence of a following reaction with $k_{2}=145 \mathrm{M}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ and $C_{\mathrm{Z}}^{*}=10 C_{\mathrm{O}}^{*}$. Points are experimental data from study of reduction of $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ in the presence of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ (85). [From K. B. Prater and A. J. Bard, J. Electrochem. Soc., 117, 1517 (1970), reprinted by permission of the publisher, The Electrochemical Society, Inc.]

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-521.jpg?height=732&width=756&top_left_y=221&top_left_x=139)
Figure 12.4.4 Variation of $n_{\text {app }}$ with $\log \left(k \omega^{-1} \nu^{1 / 3} D^{-1 / 3}\right)= \log \left(\delta^{2} / 2.6 \mu\right)$ for $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{r}}$ mechanism. Curve a: Equation 12.4.17 divided by $i_{l}$. Curve $b$ : Equation 12.4.16. Curve c: Digital simulation (83).

which gives $n_{\text {app }} / n$ upon division of both sides by $i_{l}$. Digital simulations of this reaction scheme at the RDE were also carried out (83, 89); these included cases with a second-order intervening reaction and the possibility of a disproportionation reaction between species B and C . A reaction layer treatment was also employed to relate shifts in half-wave potentials to elucidation of the ECE/DISP1 scheme (90). A comparison of the results of different treatments is given in Figure 12.4.4. A digital simulation of the RRDE behavior for this case for first- and second-order intervening reactions has been reported (83). As in other RRDE treatments of reactions with kinetic complications, the results depend on the electrode geometry. The variation of $N_{\mathrm{K}}$ with the kinetic parameters for a typical RRDE electrode (with an unperturbed collection efficiency, $N$, of 0.55 ) is shown in Figure 12.4.5.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_dc5e2a8f01d3d768799cg-521.jpg?height=551&width=804&top_left_y=1523&top_left_x=139)
Figure 12.4.5 Variation of $N_{\mathrm{K}}$ with kinetic parameters for an RRDE ( $r_{2} / r_{1}=1.13 ; r_{3} / r_{2}=1.69$ ) for the following ECE mechanism: $\mathrm{O}_{1}+n_{1} e \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{1}, \mathrm{R}_{1}+\mathrm{Z} \rightarrow \mathrm{O}_{2}+\mathrm{Y}, \mathrm{O}_{2}+n_{2} e \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{2}$, with $n_{1}=n_{2}$ and $k_{2}$ as the rate constant for the middle step. XKTC $=k_{2} C_{\mathrm{O}_{1}}^{*} \omega^{-1} \nu^{1 / 3} D^{-1 / 3}(0.51)^{-2 / 3}$; $m=C_{\mathrm{Z}}^{*} / C_{\mathrm{O}_{1}}^{*}$. Curve a: $m=0.1$. Curve $b: m=1.0$. Curve $c: m=10$. Curve d: First order. [From K. B. Prater and A. J. Bard, J. Electrochem. Soc., 117, 1517 (1970), reprinted by permission of the publisher, The Electrochemical Society, Inc.]

### 12.5 UME TECHNIQUES

The behavior of a UME depends upon the time scale of the experiment. At very short times, when $D t \ll r_{0}^{2}$, it behaves like a planar electrode. Measurements of rapid reactions are possible in this time regime, since double-layer charging effects are greatly diminished (Section 5.9.1), and the treatments of Section 12.2 apply. However, the steady-state currents or voltammograms at a UME (when $D t \gg r_{0}^{2}$ ) can also be employed to study coupled homogeneous reactions. In this case, the response is similar to that for an RDE at steady state. (The behavior in the intermediate time region is complicated and rarely used in practice). A given UME operating in the steady-state mode has the disadvantage of having only a single characteristic time ( $\sim r_{0}^{2} / D$ ) and thus a restricted time window for obtaining kinetic information. To illustrate this concept, consider the study of an ECE reaction. When the intervening reaction, (12.3.42), is fast, and the reaction layer thickness, $\mu$, is small compared to the diffusion layer thickness ( $\sim r_{0}$ ), all reactions will occur within the diffusion layer and $n_{\text {app }}=2$. When the reaction is slow, so that $\mu$ is large compared to $r_{0}$, the occurrence of (12.3.42) will have essentially no effect, and $n_{\text {app }}=1$. It is only when the reaction layer thickness is of the order of $r_{0}$ that useful kinetic information can be obtained. Thus, data at several electrodes of different $r_{0}$ must be obtained to span the needed time regime in an investigation of a single reaction, while with an RDE, a single electrode can be employed at different rotation rates.

The equations governing the steady-state current as a function of $r_{0}$ can be obtained by solving the ordinary differential equations for spherical diffusion governing the appropriate kinetic scheme or by using the reaction layer approximation (7, 91-94). The relevant behavior at microspherical electrodes in any time regime can also be obtained through digital simulation (17).

Generation/collection experiments, analogous to those at the RRDE, can be carried out with pairs of microband electrodes. In this case, the relevant time parameter is that required for the electrogenerated species to diffuse across the gap, $d$, separating the microbands (95). Because of the small spacing attainable with microbands, this time can be smaller than that typically found at the RRDE at usual rotation rates. However, the fixed gap width implies that a given pair of microbands is characterized by a narrow time window ( $\sim d^{2} / D$ ), thus requiring that a given reaction be studied at several electrode pairs. Equivalent studies of coupled reactions can be carried out by scanning electrochemical microscopy (SECM) (Section 16.4.4), where $d$ is continuously variable down to very small values (96).

### 12.6 SINE WAVE METHODS

Although there have been numerous theoretical treatments for the effects of coupled homogeneous chemical reactions on the measured faradaic impedance or ac voltammetric response, these methods generally have not found very wide application to studies of coupled chemistry. This is mainly the result of the rather complicated, often cumbersome, general equations describing the measured ac response in terms of frequency and kinetic parameters.
